main.title: Why Are You Tired All the Time?
main.description:
You slept a full eight hours, did everything right, and you're STILL a zombie by 3pm and wide awake at midnight. The reason isn't your sleep at all.

What you'll learn:
- The two clocks running your energy (and why fixing one isn't enough)
- Why your 3pm crash has nothing to do with lunch
- The "jet lag" you give yourself every weekend without leaving home
- Why waking up at 3am used to be completely normal

What time does YOUR afternoon crash hit? 3pm on the dot? Tell us below.

SOURCES
- Two-process model of sleep (sleep pressure + body clock): Borbely, Journal of Sleep Research (2016, 2022).
- Adenosine builds sleep pressure while you're awake; caffeine blocks the adenosine signal.
- The master clock (suprachiasmatic nucleus, ~20,000 cells) is set by light; evening/blue light delays it and suppresses melatonin. (StatPearls; phase-response-curve research.)
- Social jetlag: Wittmann, Roenneberg et al., Chronobiology International (2006); more than 2 in 3 people carry an hour or more per week.
- Segmented sleep ("first sleep" / "second sleep"): A. R. Ekirch, At Day's Close (historically documented, still academically debated).
- Persistent weeks-long fatigue can be medical (thyroid, low iron, sleep apnea) and is worth a doctor's visit. (Mayo Clinic; Cleveland Clinic.)

This video uses AI-assisted narration and illustrations.

#tired #sleep #circadianrhythm #socialjetlag #science #wonder

shorts.title: Waking up at 3am used to be normal 🕯️ #Shorts
shorts.description:
For most of history people slept in two shifts, a first sleep and a second sleep, with an hour awake in between. Then one invention deleted the gap.
